rows.v: A shot that captures a broad view of a scene, often to 'establish' where the action takes place. Sets the stage and shows context.
The film opened on a wide establishing shot of the city skyline before cutting to the apartment.
Descriptive: a 'wide' view, and an 'establishing' one when it sets the location. The opening wide shot orients the audience before the story narrows in.
A shot that fills the frame with a subject's face or a small detail, emphasizing emotion or importance. Intimate and revealing.
The close-up on her trembling lip told the audience everything words couldn't.
Descriptive: the camera moves 'close up' to its subject. Pioneered in early cinema, the close-up gave film its uniquely intimate emotional power.
'Panning' swivels the camera horizontally (left-right); 'tilting' swivels it vertically (up-down). Both move the view while the camera stays fixed in place.
The camera panned across the crowd, then tilted up to reveal the towering statue.
'Pan' is short for 'panorama' (a sweeping view); 'tilt' from the everyday sense of leaning. Two basic camera movements from a fixed position.
Moving the entire camera through space, usually on a wheeled 'dolly' or track. A dolly or tracking shot follows or glides alongside the action.
A slow dolly pushed in toward the actor as the tension built.
Named for the wheeled cart ('dolly') the camera rides on. Unlike a pan, the whole camera physically travels, creating immersive movement through a scene.
A shot taken from a camera mounted on a large mechanical crane or jib, allowing sweeping vertical and aerial movements high above the action.
The film ended on a soaring crane shot rising up and away from the lovers.
Named for the construction crane it resembles. The towering, swooping movement it enables is often used for grand, emotional opening or closing shots.
Point-of-View shot — framed to show what a character is seeing, putting the audience directly in their eyes. Builds immersion and identification.
The POV shot through the keyhole made the audience feel like the one spying.
An acronym for 'point of view'. By showing exactly what a character sees, it collapses the distance between audience and character into a single gaze.
A shot where the camera is deliberately tilted off-level, creating a slanted, unsettling frame. Signals tension, unease, or disorientation.
The Dutch angle on the villain's face made the whole scene feel wrong and tense.
Despite the name, it's from 'Deutsch' (German) — the technique was popularized by German Expressionist filmmakers of the 1920s to convey psychological disturbance.
How much of the image is in sharp focus from front to back. Shallow focus isolates a character; deep focus keeps everything sharp, layering the frame.
Shallow depth of field blurred the chaos behind the hero, locking the audience onto his face.
Borrowed directly from photography. In film it's a storytelling tool — directing the eye, or (in deep focus) letting the audience choose where to look.
The basic edit: an instant switch from one shot to another. The cut is the fundamental unit of film editing and pacing.
A sharp cut from the gun to the falling body let the audience's mind fill the gap.
From physically 'cutting' and splicing film strips together. The simplest transition, yet the rhythm of cuts shapes a film's entire energy and pace.
A sequence of short shots edited together to compress time or convey a lot of information quickly — like a training or falling-in-love sequence.
A montage showed the boxer's months of grueling training in ninety seconds.
French for 'assembling'. The technique of building meaning by juxtaposing shots was theorized by Soviet filmmakers and became a storytelling shorthand.
Editing that alternates between two or more scenes happening simultaneously in different places, building tension and connection between them.
Cross-cutting between the ticking bomb and the racing hero ratcheted up unbearable suspense.
Also called 'parallel editing'. By cutting back and forth, the film implies the events are happening at once, a classic engine of suspense.
An abrupt, jarring cut within a single continuous shot that breaks the smooth flow of time, often skipping forward. Can feel disorienting or stylish.
A series of jump-cuts showed her getting ready, time skipping forward in jarring leaps.
Named for the way the image seems to 'jump'. Once considered an error, it was embraced as a bold stylistic device by the French New Wave.
A cut between two shots linked by a visual or thematic similarity — a shape, motion, or idea carrying across the edit. Elegant and meaningful.
The famous match-cut leapt from a spinning bone to a spaceship, millennia passing in an instant.
Descriptive: the two shots 'match' in composition or movement, making the cut feel seamless and significant rather than abrupt.
A 'fade' transitions to or from black; a 'dissolve' blends one shot into the next as they briefly overlap. Both signal a softer passage of time than a cut.
The scene dissolved gently into the next, suggesting hours had quietly passed.
'Fade' from the image fading out; 'dissolve' from one image dissolving into another. These gentle transitions traditionally signal time or place shifts.
Consistency of details from shot to shot, so the world feels seamless — matching props, positions, and action across cuts. Errors break the illusion.
A continuity error left the actor's glass full in one shot and empty in the next.
From 'continuous' — the goal that action flows continuously despite being filmed in pieces. A dedicated crew member tracks it to avoid jarring mismatches.
Adjusting and stylizing the colors of footage in post-production to set mood, ensure consistency, and create a distinctive look.
The film's cold blue color grading gave every scene a bleak, dystopian chill.
From 'grading' film stock for color. Modern digital grading is a powerful creative tool — the teal-and-orange blockbuster look is one famous example.
The creative leader of a film, responsible for the overall vision, guiding actors, and making the key artistic decisions on set.
The director called for another take, pushing the actor toward a rawer performance.
From the sense of one who 'directs' or guides. The director is the central creative authority, shaping every department toward a unified vision.
Director of Photography (cinematographer) — the person in charge of the camera and lighting, translating the director's vision into images.
The DP chose a handheld camera and harsh lighting to give the scene a raw, documentary feel.
Also called the cinematographer, from Greek 'kinema' (movement) + 'graphein' (to write) — literally 'writing with motion', crafting the film's visual language.
The head of the lighting department on a film set, responsible for executing the lighting plan designed with the cinematographer.
The gaffer rigged the lights to cast the long, dramatic shadows the DP wanted.
From an old British word for an elder or boss ('gaffer', a contraction of 'godfather'). On set, the gaffer is the chief electrician running the lights.
The chief assistant to the gaffer (lighting) or key grip (rigging) — the second-in-command of those departments, handling logistics and crew.
The best boy coordinated the lighting crew while the gaffer focused on the setup.
An old theatrical term of uncertain origin; one story says a department head would ask another to lend their 'best boy' for a job. The title simply stuck.
The art of creating and recording everyday sound effects — footsteps, rustling, punches — in sync with the picture, in post-production.
The foley artist snapped celery to create the sound of breaking bones.
Named for sound pioneer Jack Foley, who developed the technique. These artists rebuild a film's everyday sounds from scratch, often with surprising props.
Planning and arranging the actors' movements and positions within a scene relative to the camera and set. The choreography of a scene.
They spent an hour on blocking, mapping exactly where each actor would stand and move.
From theater, where actors' positions were once marked with wooden blocks. It's the foundational staging done before lighting and shooting a scene.
The daily schedule distributed to cast and crew, listing who's needed, when, where, and what scenes are being shot. The logistical bible of a shoot.
The call sheet had her in makeup at 5 a.m. for the first scene at dawn.
Descriptive: it lists the 'call' times when each person must report. A meticulously organized document keeping a complex production running on schedule.
The classic screenplay structure dividing a story into three parts: Setup (Act 1), Confrontation (Act 2), and Resolution (Act 3). The skeleton of most films.
The script followed a tidy three-act structure, the hero hitting rock bottom right at the act two break.
A storytelling framework traced back to Aristotle's beginning-middle-end. Codified for screenwriting, it underlies the vast majority of mainstream films.
The 'protagonist' is the main character driving the story; the 'antagonist' is the force or character opposing them. The central conflict's two poles.
The protagonist's quest was thwarted at every turn by a cunning antagonist.
From Greek 'protagonistes' (first actor) and 'antagonistes' (opponent) — the lead and the one who 'struggles against' them, terms straight from ancient theater.
An object, goal, or device that drives the plot and motivates the characters, but whose specific nature is ultimately unimportant to the audience.
The secret documents were a pure MacGuffin — what mattered was the chase, not the papers.
Popularized by Alfred Hitchcock, who told a nonsense story explaining the meaningless word. It names the plot-driving thing nobody really cares about.
The background information an audience needs — who characters are, what's happened, how the world works. The challenge is delivering it without boring the viewer.
Clumsy exposition had characters explaining things they'd both obviously already know.
From Latin 'exponere' (to set forth). Skilled writers hide exposition inside conflict and action; clumsy 'info-dumps' are a common screenwriting pitfall.
Character Arc — the internal transformation a character undergoes over a story, from who they are at the start to who they become by the end.
His character arc took him from a selfish loner to a man willing to sacrifice everything.
From the geometric 'arc' (a curved path) — the character's journey curves from one state to another. A satisfying arc is the heart of memorable storytelling.
The unspoken meaning beneath the literal dialogue — what characters really mean or feel but don't say directly. The real conversation under the words.
On the surface they discussed the weather, but the subtext crackled with old resentment.
From 'sub-' (under) + 'text' — the meaning running underneath the spoken lines. Rich subtext is what separates flat dialogue from compelling drama.
Everything arranged within the frame — sets, props, lighting, costume, actor placement. The total visual composition of a shot's content.
The cluttered mise-en-scène of the hoarder's apartment told his story before a word was spoken.
French for 'placing on stage', borrowed from theater. It encompasses every visual element deliberately arranged within the frame to create meaning.
The 'martini shot' is the last shot of the day's filming. After it wraps, the crew is done — and the joke is the next shot is 'in a glass.'
The AD announced the martini shot, and the exhausted crew cheered for the final setup of the day.
Old Hollywood slang: the final shot of the day, after which everyone could go have a drink — the next 'shot' being a martini in hand.
The brief window around sunrise or sunset offering soft, warm, golden light — beloved by filmmakers but a race against fading daylight. Same as photography's golden hour.
They had only twenty minutes of magic hour to capture the romantic sunset scene.
Named for the magical quality of low-angle sunlight. 'Hour' is optimistic — the usable window is often closer to a frantic twenty or thirty minutes.
The on-set department providing snacks, drinks, and refreshments to the cast and crew throughout the shooting day. Often a crew morale barometer.
Everyone judged the production by its craft services — a well-stocked table kept the crew happy.
From 'craft' (the various crafts/trades of the crew) being 'serviced' with food. 'Crafty', as it's nicknamed, is a beloved fixture of any film set.
A brief appearance by a famous person or notable figure, often as a fun surprise or in-joke for the audience. A small but memorable role.
The director made his usual cameo, appearing for three seconds as a man on the street.
From Italian 'cammeo' (a small carved gem with a portrait) — a tiny, jewel-like appearance. The fleeting, recognizable face is the treat.
A film that is a massive commercial success, drawing huge audiences and revenue. The big-budget, crowd-pulling tentpole movie.
The summer blockbuster broke box-office records in its opening weekend.
From WWII-era 'blockbuster' bombs powerful enough to destroy a city block; applied to films whose lines 'busted the block', wrapping around the theater.
'That's a wrap' announces that filming is complete — for the day, a scene, or the whole production. The satisfying end of shooting.
After the final take, the director called 'That's a wrap!' and the set erupted in applause.
Possibly from 'wrap up' (to conclude), or a backronym for 'Wind, Reel, and Print'. Either way, it's the universal call that shooting is finished.
